passages.text: Deep Learning Can Predict the Overall Survival of Cervical Cancer Based on Histopathological Image, Gene Mutation and Clinical Information
ABSTRACT

The fusion of data features from different modes, such as pathology images and sequence data, has the potential to predict the overall survival (OS) of patients with cervical cancer. This study aims to develop a novel prediction model for overall survival (OS) that incorporates pathology images, clinical data and molecular data. The model underwent training using comprehensive cervical cancer data from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA), which include 119 patients. To independently validate the model, we used a manually collected dataset from Peking Union Medical College Hospital (PUMCH), comprising 53 patients with cervical cancer. LASSO Cox regression analysis was applied to identify relevant features associated with overall survival (OS), resulting in the identification of 484 genes, including RGR, DBN1 and CALCR, as well as numerous image features. Building upon these findings, a multimodal deep learning model was developed to effectively classify the overall survival (OS) of patients with cervical cancer into two categories: short term (ST: ≤ 3 years) and long term (LT: > 3 years) based on the integration of pathology images and clinical features. The developed model achieved reasonably good prediction accuracy in the independent testing dataset from PUMCH, with an area under the curve (AUC) value of 0.783. In conclusion, the combination of pathology images with clinical and molecular data enables the creation of accurate and reliable prediction models for cervical cancer.

This study aims to develop a novel prediction model for the overall survival of patients with cervical cancer by incorporating a comprehensive range of data, including pathology images, clinical data and molecular data. The model was trained using data from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) and subsequently validated using a dataset from Peking Union Medical College Hospital (PUMCH). The results demonstrate the model's effectiveness, achieving an area under the curve (AUC) value of 0.783. This validates that the combination of pathology images with clinical and molecular data enables the creation of accurate and reliable cervical cancer survival prediction models.
Introduction

Cervical cancer represents a substantial global health burden, being ranked as the fourth most prevalent cancer among women worldwide. It additionally ranks among the top three cancers affecting women under the age of 45. Because of the advancements in cancer screening techniques, human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccines and the identification of cancer mechanisms, such as susceptibility genes, the incidence and mortality of cervical cancer have decreased considerably. Despite these notable advancements, cervical cancer continues to impose a substantial burden, displaying a 5‐year overall survival (OS) rate of approximately 66%. Similarly to numerous other cancer types, the OS rates for patients with cervical cancer exhibit significant variations based on the stage of the cancer (https://www.cancer.net/cancer‐types/cervical‐cancer/statistics). The five‐year OS rates for patients with early‐stage cervical cancer, those with lymph node metastasis and those with distant metastasis are 92%, 44% and 18%, respectively. The significant decrease in overall survival in advanced‐stage cancer patients is primarily attributed to the high frequency of recurrence and metastasis, especially in cases where the origin is unknown. Hence, it is imperative to promptly diagnose cervical cancer and accurately assess the prognosis after resection surgery in order to determine the appropriate course of adjuvant therapy.

The standard treatment for cervical cancer typically entails neoadjuvant chemotherapy combined with surgery, supplementary surgery after radiotherapy or concurrent chemoradiotherapy. These treatment modalities have considerably enhanced the prognosis for patients with cervical cancer. Presently, clinicians depend on the International Federation of Gynaecology and Obstetrics (FIGO) staging system as a means to prognosticate patient outcomes. Studies have demonstrated a close correlation between survival rates and various factors, including tumour pathological type, degree of differentiation, primary tumour size, as well as squamous cell carcinoma antigen levels before and after treatment. Nevertheless, determining the optimal adjuvant therapy and treatment intensity for individual patients remains a challenge. Aggressive treatment approaches can pose risks and adverse effects, whereas milder treatments may not effectively prevent cancer recurrence and metastasis.

Fortunately, the progress in molecular sequencing, medical imaging and machine learning has facilitated the development of computational models that enable the evaluation of prognostic factors in patients with cervical cancer. These models consider crucial factors, including the risk of recurrence and metastasis, as well as the anticipated survival of the patient. For instance, the integration of multiomics data, such as somatic mutation, gene expression and methylation, alongside medical imaging and the cancer tissue microbiome, has been leveraged to anticipate postsurgical resection survival in cancer patients. In addition, data derived from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA), encompassing computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and whole slide pathological imaging (WSI) data, have been employed to examine overall survival in patients. Meanwhile, with the development of computer‐aided diagnosis (CAD) and image scanning technology, whole slide image (WSI) scanners have been widely used in the field of diagnostic pathology.

Multilayer neural networks, commonly referred to as deep learning, have demonstrated promising outcomes in extracting insights from diverse clinical data as well as medical images. For example, Mukhopadhyay et al. integrated deep learning algorithms with haematoxylin and eosin (H&E) images to successfully predict genetic alterations. Likewise, automated histopathological analysis systems have demonstrated their value in prognosticating various malignant tumours, such as breast cancer, lung cancer and colorectal cancer. These systems explore the correlation between the information embedded in pathological images and patients' survival. Yu et al. devised a regularised computational framework that discerns long‐term and short‐term survivors in both Stage I adenocarcinoma (p < 0.003) and squamous cell carcinoma (p = 0.023) of lung cancer patients within the TCGA dataset. This was achieved by selecting high‐level features from the entire H&E images. Sun et al. presented a pioneering approach named GPMKL that effectively integrates heterogeneous information, such as genomic data and pathological images, for predicting the survival of breast cancer patients. This method demonstrated superior performance compared to existing survival prediction methods. Zadeh et al. introduced DeepSurvNet, a deep convolutional neural network (CNN)‐based model, to precisely classify the duration of survival after diagnosis for patients diagnosed with brain cancer. This model utilises histopathological images and categorises survival length into four classes: Class I (0–6 months), Class II (6–12 months), Class III (12–24 months) and Class IV (> 24 months). Yixin Li et al. provided an overview of existing methods for pathology image analysis based on Markov random fields (MRFs) and conditional random fields (CRFs), and in the paper, they introduce the frameworks of the two random field models. They also summarised their analytical operation principles and optimisation methods. Chen Li et al., on the other hand, presented a comprehensive review: Machine vision methods for cervical histopathology image analysis are two cross‐pipelines. In the first pipeline, this article provides a longitudinal review of related studies based on the application goals of machine vision technology. In the second pipeline, this article provides a cross‐sectional review of these studies using detailed machine vision techniques. In addition, this article summarises the research motivation, methodology and system evaluation of each reference. Zizhen Fan et al. proposed a weakly supervised cervical cancer nest image recognition method based on the conjunctive attention mechanism and visual transformer (CAM‐VT), which can analyse pap slices quickly and accurately. Md Mamunur Rahaman et al. proposed a hybrid deep feature fusion (HDFF) technique based on DL to accurately classify cervical cells. In addition, Wanli Liu et al. proposed a deep learning‐based framework, CVM‐Cerebral, for the cervical cell classification task. CVM‐Cerebral first proposes a CNN module and a VT module for local and global feature extraction, respectively, and then designs a multilayer perceptron module for fusing the local and global features for final classification. Despite the growing utilisation of deep learning techniques and the analysis of histopathological images in cancer research, to the best of our knowledge, no study has endeavoured to predict the OS of patients with cervical cancer using multimodal deep learning methods that integrate histopathological images, clinical data and multiomics data. Consequently, there is a critical need for a feature fusion methodology capable of effectively integrating survival‐associated features from various data domains, including imaging data, genomic data and clinical data.

To address this research gap, we introduce a novel multimodal deep residual learning approach aimed at directly predicting the overall survival of patients with cervical cancer using H&E images and clinical information. Specifically, our contributions are as follows: (1) Multimodal deep learning model: We introduce an innovative multimodal deep residual learning model that directly predicts patients with cervical cancer' overall survival by leveraging a combination of H&E images and clinical information. This model harnesses the capabilities of deep learning to effectively integrate diverse data sources. (2) Feature fusion methodology: Addressing existing research gaps, we have developed a feature fusion methodology capable of efficiently integrating survival‐associated features from various data domains, including imaging data, genomic data and clinical data. This methodology holds the potential to enhance prognostic accuracy. (3) Independent validation: To rigorously evaluate our model's efficacy, we collected an independent dataset from Peking Union Medical College Hospital (PUMCH). This dataset comprises H&E images, clinical information and somatic mutation data, enabling us to independently validate the model's performance. (4) Clinical impact: Our research seeks to bridge the divide between computational models and clinical practice. By accurately predicting patients with cervical cancer' overall survival, our model has the potential to assist clinicians in making well‐informed decisions regarding adjuvant therapy.

In conclusion, this study presents a comprehensive approach to cervical cancer prognosis, integrating state‐of‐the‐art deep learning techniques with multimodal data fusion. We firmly believe that our contributions will advance the field of cervical cancer prognosis and, ultimately, benefit patient care.
Materials and Methods
Training Data Preparation

We evaluate the proposed methods on the public WSI dataset TCGA‐CESC. At the patient level, we randomly split the 119 prognostic patients with somatic mutations in this dataset into training and validation sets with a ratio of 80:20. The fivefold cross‐validation method was adopted to screen and determine the model hyperparameters, such as learning rate and optimiser.

Clinical characteristics of patients, including age, tumour stage, survival time, last follow‐up time and status, were compiled. The statistical standard for OS time was defined as the patient's survival time if their status was death; otherwise, it was the last follow‐up time. Patients were then categorised into two groups: ST (OS time ≤ 3 years) and LT (total survival time > 3 years). We labelled H&E images of patients with OS time of > 3 years as 0 and ≤ 3 years as 1.
Testing Data Collection and Analysis

We collected complete clinical and pathological data from 80 cases of cervical cancer at PUMCH from October 2004 to June 2012 and used these data for the independent testing of the model. The study design and sample collection were approved by the Medical Ethics Committee of Peking Union Medical College Hospital, and all participants provided informed written consent.

To be included in the study, patients had to meet the following criteria: (1) confirmation of cervical squamous cell carcinoma or adenocarcinoma by pathology; (2) confirmation of 2018 FIGO Stage Ib–IIb by at least two doctors at the level of deputy director of gynaecological oncology or above; (3) initial treatment with concurrent surgical treatment and chemoradiotherapy; (4) absence of serious heart, liver, kidney, brain or haematopoietic system diseases; (5) no history of immune‐related diseases; (6) no other tumours; and (7) complete clinical data. Patients were excluded if they had other tumours, cervical cancer of other pathological types, such as adenosquamous carcinoma or clear cell carcinoma, a history of previous radiotherapy or chemotherapy, or refused to participate in the study.
Therapy and Efficacy

The study followed up with each patient at the outpatient clinic every 3 months for 2 years and every 6 months thereafter until October 2019. Each patient was tracked for a minimum of 36 months or until death, recurrence or metastasis occurred. Throughout the follow‐up period, we assessed the long‐term efficacy of each patient by analysing their 3‐year survival rate, progression‐free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) rates. We defined the duration from the start of treatment to the last follow‐up or death as the survival time and the duration from the start of treatment to recurrence, metastasis or last follow‐up as tumour‐free survival time.
Image Preprocessing
During the preprocessing of images from TCGA and PUMCH, there are five main steps, which are detailed as follows.
Quality Control

To overcome the artefact and batch effect caused unintentionally during routine slide preparation (such as staining), there is an urgent need for a reproducible, automated method to accurately identify high‐quality slides. HistoQC is an open‐source quality control tool for digital pathological sections, which can be used to find abnormal values at the queue level—for example, slides with deeper or lighter staining than other slides in the queue. HistoQC determines abnormal slides by identifying abnormal values in image metrics (including colour histogram, brightness and contrast). Specifically, the element  of the metric matrix  represents the value of the  image metric of the  sample.

For the image metric ,  and  represent the mean and standard deviation of all samples, respectively. If , the  sample is regarded as a qualified sample; otherwise, it is regarded as an unqualified sample for filtering. Figure 1 shows four WSI samples: two WSIs that did not meet the brightness standard and were excluded (Figure 1a,c), and two WSIs that met all quality control standards and were included for tumour region annotation (Figure 1b,d).

Examples of WSIs meeting and falling short of the quality control standards. (a) A WSI excluded because the brightness was too high. (b) A WSI included because all its indicators met the quality control standards. (c) A WSI excluded because the brightness was too low. (d) A WSI included because all its indicators met the quality control standards.
Tumour Region Annotation

The tumour regions in the H&E‐stained images were marked in an irregular circle by experienced pathologists. Pathologists need to separate tumour regions from normal regions accurately, as the overall survival is related to the tumour mutations or the tumour regions. The training process was all conducted in the marked tumour regions.
Image Segmentation

WSIs are unsuitable for constructing predictive models, not only due to their significant size but also because they contain distracting areas such as voids and holes. Nonoverlapping patches of a size of 512 × 512 pixels at 40x magnification are then extracted from the tumour tissue regions.
Remove Invalid Areas

There were many invalid areas in the contour, such as areas with a high blank rate, which would have caused significant interference and would not have been conducive to model training. Before training the model, tiles with a blank ratio of more than 30% were removed.
Colour Normalisation

The method proposed by Macenko et al. was used to perform the colour normalisation of H&E images, which considered the staining technology of tissue section preparation. First, all colour values of the image were converted to optical density (OD) values using logarithmic transformation. Then, the OD tuple was decomposed by singular value decomposition to find the 2D projection with high variance. Finally, the obtained colour space transformation was applied to the original image. The computational pathology toolbox ‘tiatoolbox’ (https://github.com/TissueImageAnalytics/tiatoolbox/tree/publication) developed by Tissue Image Analytics (TIA) laboratory was used to achieve colour normalisation.

Finally, to address the sample imbalance issue in the TCGA dataset, we employed the undersampling technique during the model training process. Specifically, we randomly selected a subset of samples from the majority class (LT group) to match the sample size of the minority class (ST group), ensuring a balanced class distribution for model learning. This approach was chosen to avoid potential overfitting risks associated with oversampling (e.g., redundant information from duplicated minority samples) while maintaining the intrinsic characteristics of the original data as much as possible.
Screening of Prognostic Genes for Cervical Cancer

We used univariate Cox proportional hazards regression: Only genes with a hazard ratio (HR) 95% confidence interval (CI) not crossing 1 and a p‐value < 0.05 were retained. Additionally, to mitigate the confounding effect of clinical variables (e.g., age, FIGO staging) on survival associations, we adjusted for these covariates in the Cox model (p‐value < 0.05).
Image Feature Representation Based on ResNet18

Because the CNN has stable learning performance, it is considered to be the most advanced algorithm in image recognition and classification. The structure of CNN mainly consists of three types of layers: (1) input layer, (2) hidden layers and (3) output layer. An image is first fed into the input layer and then fed into the hidden layer for extracting low‐level features. The extracted low‐level features are fed into convolution layers and pooling layers for feature reduction and filtering, and then advanced features are extracted through the later hidden layer. Finally, the advanced features are fed to the output layer.

Residual network (ResNet) is a large‐scale CNN containing residual blocks. We used the ResNet18 convolutional neural network pretrained on the ImageNet dataset to extract image features. The ResNet18 network is composed of 18 layers, and its network structure is shown in Figure 2a. First, the tiles with 512 × 512 pixels and 3 channels (512 × 512 × 3) are resized to 224 × 224 × 3 and fed into a convolution layer with a convolution core size of 7 × 7 and a step size of 2. The output of this layer is 112 × 112, and the number of channels is 64. Then, the tile is fed into a maximum pooling layer with a convolution core size of 3 × 3 and a step size of 2, and the output of this layer is 56 × 56, and the number of channels is 64. Subsequently, there are four residual blocks, each consisting of two basic blocks, each of which contains two convolutional layers, and the structure of the residual block is shown in Figure 2b. After four residual blocks, the output size is 7 × 7, and the number of channels is 512. The last two layers of ResNet18 are the average pooling layer and the fully connected layer.

The structure of ResNet18 and residual blocks. (a) The network structure of ResNet, where yellow represents the first residual block, orange represents the second residual block, green represents the third residual block, and purple represents the fourth residual block; (b) the structure of each residual block.
Feature Fusion

Because different modes have different forms of expression and different perspectives, there are some overlaps; that is, there is information redundancy, or there may be a variety of information interactions between different modes. If we can analyse multimodal information reasonably, we can obtain rich feature information, which will provide theoretical support for improving prediction performance. Therefore, we considered fusing the two modes of H&E images and clinical information to improve the OS prediction of patients with cervical cancer.
We used multimodal compact bilinear (MCB) for feature fusion. The expression of MCB is as follows:

In the equation,  represents the features from different modalities of input data;  and  denote the weights learned by the model from images and clinical information, respectively. Correspondingly,  and  represent features from two different modalities of data.  represents the number of generated .

MCB uses a pretrained CNN to extract high‐level features from images and word embeddings from text, and then it decodes the extracted features through long short‐term memory. It should be pointed out that word embedding can convert text into digital representation, helping the model to better process text information. Then, the count sketch method is used to reduce the dimension of the features. After obtaining the reduced‐dimension features, a fast Fourier transform (FFT) is used to transform the features to the frequency domain, the vector inner product is performed in the frequency domain, and the inner product result is inversely transformed to the time domain space by FFT. Specifically, the MCB algorithm was used to fuse the penultimate layer of ResNet18 with clinical features, and the fusion result was fed into the BN layer before the linear layer. The feature dimension of the clinical part is 1, with the clinical feature being age, and the feature dimension from the ResNet part is 128. These two features together serve as inputs to the MCB module, which then outputs features with a size of 128.
Nuclear Segmentation and Classification

To bolster the model's interpretability, we conducted an investigation into the make‐up of the tumour microenvironment in patients. Here, for a given patch, we use HoVer‐Net trained on the PanNuke dataset to locate cell nuclei and predict their types. HoVer‐Net is a convolutional neural network that performs segmentation and classification of cell nuclei. This network leverages instance‐rich information encoded within vertical and horizontal distance maps of nuclear pixels to their centres of mass and achieves accurate segmentation even in areas with overlapping instances. Five categories of nuclei are predicted: neoplastic, non‐neoplastic epithelial, inflammatory, connective tissue and dead cells. For each image patch, HoVer‐Net generates a set of nuclear centroids together with their cell type and the corresponding nuclear segmentation mask.
Nuclear Morphological Features Extraction

Assuming that the morphology of different types of nuclei is associated with OS time, we extract 15 nuclear morphological features, such as nuclear size, eccentricity, orientation and length of the major axis (see Table 1), using the output binary mask of each nucleus. Therefore, each detected nucleus is represented by a 15‐dimensional feature vector which contains 15 different morphological properties. We utilised the average values and standard deviations of 15 features for the five types of cell nuclei, as well as the counts of the five types of cell nuclei, to obtain a 155‐dimensional feature vector for each patch.
Overview of 15 quantitatively measured shape descriptors for assessing cell morphology.

FeatureDescription
AreaNumber of pixels in the region
bbox_areaThe pixel count of the bounding box that encloses the target region
convex_areaThe number of pixels in the convex hull image, where the convex hull refers to the smallest convex polygon that encloses the target region
EccentricityRatio of the focal distance (distance between focal points) over the major axis length
equivalent_diameterThe diameter of a circle with the same area as the region
euler_numberNumber of objects in the region subtracted from the number of holes in those objects
ExtentThe proportion of pixels in the bounding box that are also in the region
filler_areaNumber of pixels of the region with all the holes filled in
inertia_tensor_eigvals_xThe eigenvalues of the inertia tensor along the x‐axis in decreasing order
inertia_tensor_eigvals_yThe eigenvalues of the inertia tensor along the y‐axis in decreasing order
major_axis_lengthThe length of the major axis of the ellipse that has the same normalised second central moments as the region
minor_axis_lengthThe length of the minor axis of the ellipse that has the same normalised second central moments as the region
OrientationThe angle between the x‐axis and the major axis of the ellipse, where the ellipse shares the same second moments as the target region
PerimeterPerimeter of object which approximates the contour as a line through the centres of border pixels using a 4‐connectivity
SolidityThe ratio of the region's pixel count to the convex hull image's pixel count

Statistical Analysis

We obtained the OS time of all patients in the training cohort and test cohort and estimated the survival probability of patients with cervical cancer using the Kaplan–Meier method. We then plotted the Kaplan–Meier survival curve to estimate the 50th percentile (median) of survival time and compare the survival distribution of two or more groups. The differences in survival among groups were compared by the log‐rank test with a p‐value of < 0.05 indicating significant difference. The data were analysed using R software. In each cohort, patients with cervical cancer were divided into two classes (Class ST: OS time ≤ 3 years; Class LT: OS time > 3 years) to complete the following assessment. We used Cox survival analysis to measure the influencing factors of survival on somatic mutation data, which was realised by the R package ‘survival’ and visualised by the function ‘ggforest’ in the ‘surviviner’ package. The GO and KEGG enrichment of survival‐associated genes were analysed and plotted using the R package ‘clusterprofiler’. In addition, the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve and the area under the curve (AUC) were calculated to evaluate and verify the discrimination and stability of the model for predicting the overall survival of cervical cancer.
Results
Framework of the Study

The study employed a framework depicted in Figure 3 to examine biomarkers relevant to the overall survival (OS) of patients with cervical cancer. A multimodal deep learning approach was developed to forecast OS. In this study, haematoxylin and eosin (H&E) images from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) and Peking Union Medical College Hospital (PUMCH) were scanned at a resolution of 0.12 microns per pixel under a 40x objective lens. The quality of the scanned images was controlled, and the tumour area was labelled by pathologists (Figure 3a). Our proposed framework comprises three parts: data preprocessing, model training and model inference. During data preprocessing, the tumour regions of H&E images were segmented into patches (512 × 512 pixels), and colour normalisation was performed. Then, we used univariate Cox regression analysis and KM analysis to screen for mutation features and clinical features associated with survival, respectively. Finally, we used the random forest method to model the screened mutations or clinical characteristics related to survival and ranked them according to their importance. These three parts of data constitute the dataset for our model training. The model training was verified using fivefold cross‐validation to obtain the best hyperparameters and model structure (Figure 3b). The best models and hyperparameters were then retrained on the TCGA dataset and inferred on the PUMCH dataset (Figure 3c). H&E image features were extracted utilising ResNet18, and a multimodal model for predicting the survival of patients with cervical cancer was established by integrating the H&E image features with the molecular features (Figure 3d).

Framework of this study. (a) Data annotation: H&E‐stained slides were scanned, and the pathologist was asked to label the quality and tumour area. (b) Cross‐validation on TCGA: The data of the three modes are preprocessed as input to the model, and the hyperparameters and model structure are selected using cross‐validation. (c) Train and inference: Full TCGA data are used for training and PUMCH data for inference. (d) Building a novel fusion model to predict overall survival.
Our proposed model has a floating point operation per second (flop) of 7294.15 million, whereas the number of parameters is only 22.42 million.
Patient Characteristics

A total of 251 cases of patients with cervical cancer were downloaded from TCGA, comprising H&E images, clinical information and somatic mutation data obtained through WES. Additionally, 65 cases from PUMCH were collected. Patients with unavailable survival information (123 from TCGA and two from PUMCH) were excluded. HistoQC was applied for quality control, resulting in the removal of 19 patients (nine from TCGA and 10 from PUMCH). Consequently, our study comprised 119 patients from TCGA and 53 patients from PUMCH. Table 2 presents an overview of the patients' general characteristics. There were slight differences in the distribution of clinical characteristics between TCGA and PUMCH patients. Specifically, in the TCGA dataset, 64 (53.78%) patients were < 50 years old, whereas 55 (46.22%) patients were ≥ 50 years old. Furthermore, over 50% of the patients were classified as Stage I, with 23 (19.33%) at Stage II, 17 (14.26%) at Stage III and 17 (14.26%) at Stage IV. Based on the duration of OS, patients were divided into two groups: ST (≤ 3 years) and LT (> 3 years). Of the patients, 40 (33.61%) had an OS time of ≤ 3 years, whereas the remaining 79 (66.39%) patients had an OS time of > 3 years. Moving on to the PUMCH dataset, 42 (79.25%) patients were < 50 years old, and all samples were early‐stage tumours, with 32 (60.38%) patients at Stage I and 21 (39.62%) patients at Stage II.
Slide‐level classification using fivefold cross‐validation in TCGA datasets.

Tumour Stage Is Significantly Associated With Overall Survival Rate in Patients With Cervical Cancer

Survival analysis was carried out to assess the impact of clinical characteristics, and the corresponding survival curves were generated using the training data, as depicted in Figure 4. Age was stratified into two groups: 0 represented individuals younger than 50 years old, whereas 1 included individuals aged 50 years or older. The analysis revealed that age did not have a significant impact on the survival rate (log‐rank p = 0.32) (Figure 4a). On the other hand, a statistically significant difference in survival rate was observed among patients at different tumour stages (log‐rank p = 0.0056) (Figure 4b).
Kaplan–Meier plot of clinical features. (a) Kaplan–Meier curves of the groups with patients < 50 years old and ≥ 50 years old. (b) Kaplan–Meier curves of tumour stage.
Some Differentially Mutated Genes Were Associated With Overall Survival Rate in Patients With Cervical Cancer

To identify genetic mutations that have an impact on overall survival, a univariate Cox survival analysis was conducted using LASSO Cox regression. In order to mitigate the influence of clinical variables, a proportional hazards regression model was established with age and stage as covariates. Consequently, a total of 484 genes were discovered to exhibit significant associations with the survival rate. As shown in Figure 5a–c, the hazard ratios (HRs) of the top three genes significantly associated with the survival rate were determined to be as follows: RGR (HR = 75.3, 95% CI = 12.98–436.6, p < 0.001), DBN1 (HR = 35.4, 95% CI = 7.22–173.9, p < 0.001) and CALCR (HR = 35.8, 95% CI = 6.89–185.6, p < 0.001***). Mutations occurring in these genes are linked to an elevated risk of mortality.

Establishment of the differentially mutated genes prediction model associated with the OS of cervical cancer and OS‐related gene enrichment analysis. (a–c). Hazard ratios of the enrolled OS‐related genes obtained using LASSO Cox regression analysis. (d) Enriched GO terms for biological process annotations. (e) Enriched KEGG pathways.

Functional enrichment analysis was conducted on the 484 genes found to be significantly different. The results of the Gene Ontology (GO) pathway enrichment analysis revealed enrichments in various pathways, including ‘mitotic sister chromatid segregation’, ‘negative regulation of peptidyl‐tyrosine phosphorylation’, ‘mitotic nuclear division’, ‘negative regulation of transferase activity’, ‘nuclear division’, ‘sister chromatid segregation’, ‘organelle fission’, ‘establishment of organelle localisation’, ‘negative regulation of kinase activity’, and ‘chromatin‐mediated maintenance of transcription’ (Figure 5d). Additionally, the Kyoto Encyclopaedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathway enrichment analysis demonstrated significant enrichments, primarily in pathways related to ‘Olfactory transduction,’ ‘Other types of O‐glycan biosynthesis,’ ‘Ether lipid metabolism,’ ‘Glycosphingolipid biosynthesis‐globo and isoglobo series,’ ‘Phenylalanine metabolism,’ ‘Cushing syndrome,’ ‘Nicotine addiction,’ and ‘Other glycan degradation’ (Figure 5e).
Histopathological Images Can Predict Cervical Cancer Overall Survival Better

To enhance the precision of cervical cancer survival prediction, we developed prediction models utilising clinical data, molecular data and H&E image data, all based on a dedicated training set. The clinical and gene mutation models were constructed employing a random forest approach, with hyperparameters determined through grid search. To maintain data integrity, special attention was given to the H&E image data to prevent information leakage. Tiles extracted from the same patient were classified into separate datasets. Each tile was then processed by ResNet18, traversing the convolution and pooling layers to obtain the probability of the tile. By averaging the probabilities of all tiles, we estimated the probability of each slide, subsequently assigning the patient to the ST cohort if the projected probability exceeded 0.5 and to the LT cohort otherwise.

The receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves, generated through fivefold cross‐validation, are presented in Figure 6a. Remarkably, the performance of the prediction model for cervical cancer survival based on H&E images exhibited the highest accuracy, achieving an area under the curve (AUC) value of 0.725. In comparison, the predictive performance of using clinical data yielded an AUC of 0.647, whereas molecular data yielded the lowest predictive performance with an AUC of 0.559.

Performance of overall survival prediction models. (a) ROC curves of models based on H&E images, clinical data, molecular data, H&E images combined with molecular data and H&E images combined with clinical data on the training set. (b) ROC curves of models based on H&E images and H&E images combined with clinical data on the independent test set. (c) Bar chart of AUC, precision and recall based on H&E images and H&E images combined with clinical data on the independent test set. (d) Confusion matrix on the independent test set.
Comparison With Baselines and Different Data Fusions

Classification results are summarised in Table 2. Additionally, we evaluated several other classic ResNet methods, including ResNet50 and ResNet101. To investigate the impact of multimodal information on cervical cancer survival, we combined clinical or molecular features with image patch features for modelling. The ablation experiments of MCBP and Concat multimodal fusion methods were conducted based on the ResNet backbone.

The results indicate that the integration of ResNet18 with clinical features brought about a certain improvement under the AUC metric. Nevertheless, owing to the constraints on sample size, there exists a subtle difference in mutation distribution and patient survival. This adds interference factors to the model when mutation information is introduced.
Multimodal Information Based on the Combination of Clinical Data and H&E Images Can Improve the Accuracy of Predicting Cervical Cancer Survival

To investigate the impact of the fusion model, the top three molecular features determined through feature importance ranking were combined with image features. The performance of this fused model, evaluated using fivefold cross‐validation, is presented in Figure 6a. The results reveal that the AUC for H&E images combined with the KLHDC7B molecular feature was 0.686, whereas the AUC for H&E images combined with the age feature was 0.738 on the training set. Figure 6b displays the ROC curve of the fusion model on an independent test set, with an AUC of 0.720 for H&E images and 0.783 for H&E images combined with the age feature. Additionally, the precision and recall of the model were evaluated on independent test sets (Figure 6c), indicating that the precision and recall for H&E images combined with the age feature were 0.610 and 0.636, respectively, both higher than those for H&E images alone. The confusion matrix of this model on the independent test set is illustrated in Figure 6d. The results demonstrate that incorporating clinical features enhances the accuracy of the model's predictions. However, the model's predictive performance based on H&E images combined with gene mutation features was inferior to that of the model based solely on H&E images, potentially due to the fact that the introduced gene mutation features contained less valuable information and introduced more noise. Nonetheless, the multimodal information derived from the combination of clinical data and H&E images can effectively forecast the survival rate of patients with cervical cancer.
Visualisation of Detection Results

To further decipher the biological basis of the model's decision‐making, we employed gradient‐weighted class activation mapping (Grad‐CAM) for visual analysis of the trained predictive model. By generating heat maps, we localised key regions within the input features that contribute significantly to the model's classification, thereby revealing the core biological features prioritised by the model. As shown in Figure 7, in the ST group, as shown by the heat maps, our model focuses more on the stromal regions and relatively fewer tumour cells. In contrast, the heat maps of the LT group pay greater attention to areas with dense tumour cells.
Visualisation of model. Left: the heat maps represent the ST group; right: the heat maps represent the LT group.
Correlation Between Tumour Microenvironment and Overall Survival

To ascertain the impact of the nuclear morphological features of the five types of extracted cell nuclei on overall survival, Cox multivariate survival analyses were conducted. As shown in Figure 8a–b, the C‐index values for the Cox multivariate models constructed with 31 features of inflammatory and dead cells in TCGA are all 0.76. The C‐index values for neoplastic, non‐neoplastic epithelial and connective tissue are 0.77, 0.76 and 0.78 (see Figure S1), respectively. Figure 8c displays that, with the exception of neoplastic, a statistically significant difference in survival rates was observed among patients for the other four types of cells (log‐rank p < 0.05). For the Cox multivariate models trained on various cell types in TCGA, validation using the PUMCH datasets revealed the following C‐index values: Neoplastic cells had a C‐index of 0.49, non‐neoplastic epithelial cells had a C‐index of 0.57, inflammatory cells had a C‐index of 0.62, connective tissue had a C‐index of 0.49, and dead cells had a C‐index of 0.64 (Figure 8d).

Development and validation of predictive model for survival‐related morphological features of cell nuclei. (a–b) The forest plot demonstrating the Cox multivariate model constructed for inflammatory and necrotic cell nuclei features in TCGA. (c) The Kaplan–Meier curves show the effect of high and low levels of each type of cell content on survival. (d) The bar graphs show the validated C‐index values for five cell types in PUMCH.
Discussion

Cervical cancer presents a significant global public health concern, ranking as the fourth most prevalent cancer among women worldwide. It is particularly burdensome in low‐ and middle‐income countries compared to high‐income countries. Clinicians grapple with the challenge of selecting the most suitable treatment approach for each patient, emphasising the importance of survival rate analysis in clinical decision‐making. The identification of biomarkers capable of predicting the survival outcome in patients with cervical cancer would allow clinicians to tailor treatment plans, avoiding unnecessary interventions in cases of stable conditions. Nevertheless, the development of an effective prognostic model for survival prediction remains a major obstacle. Recent advances in computer technology have given rise to computer‐aided diagnostic and prognostic prediction methods predicated on H&E‐stained images. Despite these advancements, further improvement is needed to enhance the accuracy of image‐based prognostic prediction models.

In this study, we have developed a multimodal deep learning algorithm to directly predict the overall survival of patients with cervical cancer using H&E‐stained histology and clinical informatics. Our dataset consisted of 119 samples from TCGA and 53 samples from PUMCH, encompassing WSIs, clinical information and somatic mutation data based on WES. Our survival analysis demonstrated statistically significant differences in overall survival among patients with different tumour stages, with Stage IV patients exhibiting the poorest survival rate (Figure 4b). Early detection and systematic treatment have considerably improved the prognosis for early‐stage cervical cancer. However, in advanced‐stage cervical cancer, tumour progression and recurrence have led to a high mortality rate. To identify molecular markers associated with cervical cancer survival, we conducted univariate Cox survival analysis using LASSO Cox regression. Among the 484 genes found to be significantly associated with overall survival, RGR, DBN1 and CALCR were the three most significant based on Cox analysis (p ≤ 0.01). We conducted a literature search for the 484 mutated genes identified in our study and found that 102 of them have been previously reported to be associated with cervical cancer. This confirms the reliability of our use of Cox analysis for screening mutated genes. DBN1 has been reported to be overexpressed in colorectal cancer and plays a crucial role in its metastasis. In a study focused on non‐small‐cell lung cancer patients in Stages I to III, a gene prognostic model incorporating DBN1 as a multivariate marker showed potential clinical value for risk stratification. CALCR, on the other hand, belongs to the G protein‐coupled neuropeptide receptor family and has been linked to poor prognosis in acute myeloid leukaemia (AML) and is upregulated at AML relapse. Additionally, Xing et al. found a decreased expression of CALCRL in patients with cervical squamous cell carcinoma and endocervical adenocarcinoma. Interestingly, patients with low CALCRL expression exhibited better OS. These studies further support the close association between CALCRL and cancer. In the functional enrichment analysis of the three genes, the association between the KEGG pathway ‘olfactory transduction’ and cervical cancer prognosis is strong. Liu et al. revealed that OLFM4 augmentation interferes with the mTOR signalling pathway, and the suppressive effects of OLFM4 on cell migration and invasion are largely weakened by phosphatidic acid (PA)‐induced mTOR signal activation, which implicates the potential role of the mTOR pathway in OLFM4‐related cervical metastasis. Our findings underscore the contribution of molecular markers in determining overall survival of cervical cancer. Nevertheless, further investigations and confirmation from prospective studies are necessary.

We subsequently developed a deep learning model by utilising H&E images, clinical information and molecular data from 119 patients with cervical cancer sourced from TCGA. To evaluate the model's performance, we employed an independent test set comprising 53 patients with cervical cancer from PUMCH. Our findings revealed that H&E histopathological images exhibited the highest predictive power, achieving an AUC of 0.725. To further enhance the prediction performance, we integrated H&E image features with other omics features. Notably, the multimodal deep learning model, which combined H&E images with clinical features, exhibited the most accurate predictions, attaining an AUC of 0.783 on the independent test set. These results strongly suggest the potential of multimodal deep learning models as promising tools for accurately predicting overall survival in cervical cancer.

Furthermore, to enhance the model's interpretability, we hypothesised that the micromorphology of cells within H&E‐stained slides could predict biological prognosis. To this end, we extracted morphological features of five types of cell nuclei for Cox multivariate survival analysis. We found that inflammatory and dead cells may be associated with patient survival. Mu et al. also suggested that preoperative systemic inflammation scores are a simple yet useful prognostic factor for postoperative survival in patients with cervical cancer. Meanwhile, Liu et al. showed that a cell death‐related lncRNA‐based index could be used to predict the prognosis of patients with cervical cancer, especially the sensitivity to chemotherapeutic agents. This confirms that there is a potential link between H&E and OS.

However, our study does have several limitations. Firstly, the sample size we utilised was relatively small, and the clinical information collected was limited in scope. Secondly, the accuracy of our model requires further improvement. Thirdly, notable variations in race and genetic backgrounds existed between the training and test data sources. Finally, hyperspectral and multispectral imaging of cell and tissue autofluorescence is an emerging technology in which fluorescence imaging is applied to biological materials across multiple spectral channels. In future research endeavours, we intend to address these limitations by increasing the number of patients with cervical cancer included in our study, incorporating a broader range of clinical features and implementing more advanced hyperspectral and multispectral imaging and deep learning techniques to enhance the model's accuracy. Additionally, we aim to explore potential disparities in survival rates, H&E image characteristics and gene mutation profiles among different populations.
Conclusion

In summary, our study has successfully identified molecular markers that are significantly linked to the overall survival of patients with cervical cancer. Notably, we have demonstrated the potential of utilising H&E‐stained histopathological images as a means to accurately predict survival outcomes. Furthermore, our findings highlight the added benefit of combining H&E image features with relevant clinical features via a deep learning model, leading to improved prediction performance. However, it is crucial to note that the clinical validity of our model necessitates confirmation through rigorous experimentation in future research pursuits.

In our future investigations, we intend to explore alternative combinations of H&E image features and omics data to construct an even more precise prediction model for the overall survival of patients with cervical cancer. Furthermore, the successful implementation of deep learning in this context has the potential to revolutionise routine clinical practice.

Supporting information
Data Availability Statement

WSI (.svs file), clinical data and mutation data from the TCGA‐CESC cohort are publicly available and can be downloaded from the GDC Data Portal. The PUMCH cohort used for external validation in this study has not been made publicly available due to privacy law restrictions. The relevant data and tumour samples of this cohort are held by the first author of this article (J. S.). All requests for sharing data and samples must be submitted to the corresponding author within 15 years from the date of publication of this article, accompanied by a scientific research proposal. Based on the specific research proposal, the first author, J. S., will determine the timing, duration, specific purposes and conditions for data sharing upon obtaining ethical approval. The processing period for data access requests is 3 months.
